From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [War cry](/w/index.php?title=War_cry&redirect=no "War cry"))

Yell or chant taken up in battle

For other uses, see [Battle cry (disambiguation)](/wiki/Battle_cry_(disambiguation) "Battle cry (disambiguation)") and [War cry (disambiguation)](/wiki/War_cry_(disambiguation) "War cry (disambiguation)").

"War chant" redirects here. For the stallion with the same name, see [War Chant](/wiki/War_Chant "War Chant"). For other uses, see [war song](/wiki/War_song "War song").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/MC_09-0081-354_-_Flickr_-_NZ_Defence_Force.jpg/250px-MC_09-0081-354_-_Flickr_-_NZ_Defence_Force.jpg)](/wiki/File:MC_09-0081-354_-_Flickr_-_NZ_Defence_Force.jpg)

[NZDF](/wiki/New_Zealand_Defence_Force "New Zealand Defence Force") soldiers performing a battle cry

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Festiwal_Polka_1_Fot.Wojtek_Korpusik.jpg/250px-Festiwal_Polka_1_Fot.Wojtek_Korpusik.jpg)](/wiki/File:Festiwal_Polka_1_Fot.Wojtek_Korpusik.jpg)

A [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") performer giving a [Haka](/wiki/Haka "Haka") at a folk festival in Poland

A **battle cry** or **war cry** is a yell or chant taken up in [battle](/wiki/Battle "Battle"), usually by members of the same combatant group.
Battle cries are not necessarily articulate (e.g. "Eulaliaaaa!", "[Alala](/wiki/Alala "Alala")"..), although they often aim to invoke patriotic or religious sentiment. Their purpose is twofold, both arousing aggression and [esprit de corps](/wiki/Esprit_de_corps "Esprit de corps") on one's own side and causing intimidation on the hostile side. Battle cries are a universal form of [display behaviour](/wiki/Display_behaviour "Display behaviour") (i.e., [threat display](/wiki/Threat_display "Threat display")) aiming at [competitive advantage](/wiki/Competitive_advantage "Competitive advantage"), ideally by overstating one's own aggressive potential to a point where the enemy prefers to avoid confrontation altogether and opts to flee. In order to overstate one's potential for aggression, battle cries need to be as loud as possible, and have historically often been amplified by acoustic devices such as [horns](/wiki/Blowing_horn "Blowing horn"), [drums](/wiki/War_drum "War drum"), [conches](/wiki/Conch "Conch"), [carnyxes](/wiki/Carnyx "Carnyx"), [bagpipes](/wiki/Bagpipe "Bagpipe"), [bugles](/wiki/Bugle_(instrument) "Bugle (instrument)"), etc. (see also [martial music](/wiki/Martial_music "Martial music")).

Battle cries are closely related to other behavioral patterns of [human aggression](/wiki/Human_aggression "Human aggression"), such as [war dances](/wiki/War_dance "War dance") and taunting, performed during the "warming up" phase preceding the escalation of physical violence. From the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), many cries appeared on [speech scrolls](/wiki/Speech_scroll "Speech scroll") in [standards](/wiki/Heraldic_flag "Heraldic flag") or [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") as slogans (see [slogan (heraldry)](/wiki/Slogan_(heraldry) "Slogan (heraldry)")) and were adopted as [mottoes](/wiki/Motto "Motto"), an example being the motto "[Dieu et mon droit](/wiki/Dieu_et_mon_droit "Dieu et mon droit")" ("God and my right") of the English kings. It is said that this was [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III_of_England "Edward III of England")'s rallying cry during the [Battle of Crécy](/wiki/Battle_of_Cr%C3%A9cy "Battle of Crécy"). The word "[slogan](/wiki/Slogan "Slogan")" originally derives from *sluagh-gairm* or *sluagh-ghairm* (*sluagh* = "people", "army", and *gairm* = "call", "proclamation"), the [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic") word for "gathering-cry" and in times of war for "battle-cry". The Gaelic word was borrowed into English as *slughorn*, *sluggorne*, "slogum", and *slogan*.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Battle_cry&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Battle_cry&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Antiquity")]

* The war cry is an aspect of epic battle in [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer"): in the *[Iliad](/wiki/Iliad "Iliad")*, [Diomedes](/wiki/Diomedes "Diomedes") is conventionally called "Diomedes of the loud war cry." [Hellenes](/wiki/Hellenes "Hellenes") and [Akkadians](/wiki/Akkadians "Akkadians") alike uttered the [onomatopoeic](/wiki/Onomatopoeia "Onomatopoeia") cry *"[alala](/wiki/Alala "Alala")"* in battle.[[1]](#cite_note-1)
* The troops of ancient [Athens](/wiki/Ancient_Athens "Ancient Athens"), during the [Medic Wars](/wiki/Greco-Persian_Wars "Greco-Persian Wars") and the [Peloponnesian War](/wiki/Peloponnesian_War "Peloponnesian War") were noted for going into battle shouting "*Alala!"* or *"Alale!*", which was supposed to emulate the cry of the owl, the bird of their patron goddess [Athena](/wiki/Athena "Athena").[[2]](#cite_note-2)
* The [Western Huns](/wiki/Western_Huns "Western Huns") attacked with terrifying battle cries.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
* One of the common [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") war cries was "[Hara Hara Mahadeva](/wiki/Hara_Hara_Mahadeva "Hara Hara Mahadeva")" meaning, "*Hail to Mahadeva!" ([Shiva](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva")).*[[4]](#cite_note-4)
* Another common war cry in [India](/wiki/India "India") was "Jai Bhavani" meaning, "*Hail goddess [Bhavani](/wiki/Bhavani "Bhavani")!”*
* A common war cry used in ancient [Tamilakam](/wiki/Tamilakam "Tamilakam") was "Vetrivel, Veeravel", meaning, "Victorious vel, courageous vel." [Vel](/wiki/Vel "Vel") is the spear of [Murugan](/wiki/Murugan "Murugan"), the regional form of [Kartikeya](/wiki/Kartikeya "Kartikeya"), the Hindu war deity. In the contemporary period, the battle cry "Vetrivel, Veeravel" is being used in the 191 Field Regiment of the [Indian Army](/wiki/Indian_Army "Indian Army") based in [Madukkarai](/wiki/Madukkarai "Madukkarai"), [Coimbatore](/wiki/Coimbatore "Coimbatore").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Battle_cry&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

|  |
| --- |
| Part of a [series](/wiki/Category:Heraldry "Category:Heraldry") on |
| [Heraldic](/wiki/Heraldry "Heraldry") [achievement](/wiki/Achievement_(heraldry) "Achievement (heraldry)") |
| External devices in addition to the central [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") |
| **[Escutcheon](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)")**  **[Field](/wiki/Field_(heraldry) "Field (heraldry)")**  **[Supporter](/wiki/Supporter "Supporter")**  **[Crest](/wiki/Crest_(heraldry) "Crest (heraldry)")**  **[Torse](/wiki/Torse "Torse")**  **[Mantling](/wiki/Mantling "Mantling")**  **[Helmet](/wiki/Helmet_(heraldry) "Helmet (heraldry)")**  **[Crown](/wiki/Crown_(heraldry) "Crown (heraldry)")**  **[Compartment](/wiki/Compartment_(heraldry) "Compartment (heraldry)")**  **[Charge](/wiki/Charge_(heraldry) "Charge (heraldry)")**  **[Motto](/wiki/Motto_(heraldry) "Motto (heraldry)")** (or **[slogan](/wiki/Slogan_(heraldry) "Slogan (heraldry)")**)  **[Coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms")** [Outline of a coat of arms](/wiki/File:Coat-elements_transparent.svg) |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Azure,_a_bend_Or.svg) [Heraldry portal](/wiki/Portal:Heraldry "Portal:Heraldry") |
| * [v](/wiki/Template:Heraldic_achievement "Template:Heraldic achievement") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Heraldic_achievement "Template talk:Heraldic achievement") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Heraldic_achievement "Special:EditPage/Template:Heraldic achievement") |

* During the Scottish wars of independence, Scottish soldiers used [Alba gu bràth](/wiki/Alba_gu_br%C3%A0th "Alba gu bràth") as a battle-cry, a phrase that means 'Scotland for ever' (literally, 'Scotland until judgement'.) This was depicted in the film *[Braveheart](/wiki/Braveheart "Braveheart")* during which [Mel Gibson](/wiki/Mel_Gibson "Mel Gibson"), playing [William Wallace](/wiki/William_Wallace "William Wallace"), shouts the phrase to rally his soldiers just before a battle commenced.
* Each Turkic tribe and tribal union had its distinct [tamga](/wiki/Tamga "Tamga") (seal), totemic *[ongon](/wiki/Ongon "Ongon")* bird, and distinct *uran* (battle cry) (hence the Slavic *urah* "battle cry").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) While tamgas and *ongons* could be distinct down to individuals, the hue of horses and *uran* battle cries belonged to each tribe, were passed down from generation to generation, and some modern battle cries were recorded in antiquity. On split of the tribe, their unique distinction passed to a new political entity, endowing different modern states with the same *uran* battle cries of the split tribes, for example [Kipchak](/wiki/Kipchak_people "Kipchak people") battle cry among [Kazakhs](/wiki/Kazakhs "Kazakhs"), [Kirgizes](/wiki/Kyrgyz_people "Kyrgyz people"), [Turkmens](/wiki/Turkmens "Turkmens"), and [Uzbeks](/wiki/Uzbeks "Uzbeks"). Some larger tribes' *uran* battle cries:
  + Kipchak – "ay-bas" ("*lunar head*").[[9]](#cite_note-9)
  + Kangly (Kangars) – "bai-terek" ("*sacred tree*").[[10]](#cite_note-10)
  + Oguzes – "teke" ("*mount*")[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* [Desperta ferro!](/wiki/Awake_iron! "Awake iron!") ("Awake iron!" in Catalan) was the most characteristic cry of the [Almogavar](/wiki/Almogavar "Almogavar") warriors during the [Crown of Aragon](/wiki/Crown_of_Aragon "Crown of Aragon") military campaigns across the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") from the 12th through to the 14th centuries.
* [Deus vult](/wiki/Deus_vult "Deus vult")! ("God wills it!" in Latin) was the battle cry of the Crusaders.
* [Montjoie Saint Denis!](/wiki/Montjoie_Saint_Denis! "Montjoie Saint Denis!"): battle cry of the [Kings of France](/wiki/Kings_of_France "Kings of France") since the 12th century.
* [Santiago y cierra, España!](/wiki/Santiago! "Santiago!") was a war cry of Spanish troops during the [Reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista"), and of the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire").
* On 14 August 1431, the whole [Army of the Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Army_of_the_Holy_Roman_Empire "Army of the Holy Roman Empire") (of the [4th anti-Hussite crusade](/wiki/Hussite_Wars "Hussite Wars")) was defeated by the [Hussites](/wiki/Hussites "Hussites") in the [Battle of Domažlice](/wiki/Battle_of_Doma%C5%BElice "Battle of Domažlice"). Attacking imperial units started to retreat after hearing [Ktož jsú boží bojovníci](/wiki/Kto%C5%BE_js%C3%BA_bo%C5%BE%C3%AD_bojovn%C3%ADci "Ktož jsú boží bojovníci") ("*Ye Who Are Warriors of God*") choral and were annihilated shortly after.
* *[Allāhu Akbar](/wiki/Allahu_Akbar "Allahu Akbar")* (الله أكبر, "[God](/wiki/God_in_Islam "God in Islam") is [the] Greatest") and *Allāhu Allāh* (الله الله, "God! God!") were used by Muslim armies throughout history. *Al-naṣr aw al-shahāda* (النصر أو الشهادة, "Victory or martyrdom") was also a common battle cry; the [At-Tawbah](/wiki/At-Tawbah "At-Tawbah") 9:52 says that God has promised to the righteous Muslim warrior one of these two glorious ideals.[*[original research?](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")*]
* *Óðinn á yðr alla* (*[Odin](/wiki/Odin "Odin") owns you all*) – A reference to Odin's self sacrifice at Yggdrasil. Attributed to [Eric the Victorious](/wiki/Eric_the_Victorious "Eric the Victorious").

### Pre-modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Battle_cry&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Pre-modern")]

* When suppressing peasants' rebellions in Germany and Scandinavia around 1500, such as in the [Battle of Hemmingstedt](/wiki/Battle_of_Hemmingstedt "Battle of Hemmingstedt"), the Dutch mercenaries of the [Black Guard](/w/index.php?title=Schwarze_Garde&action=edit&redlink=1 "Schwarze Garde (page does not exist)") yelled *Wahr di buer, die garde kumt* ("Beware, peasants, the guards are coming"). When the peasants counterattacked, they responded with *Wahr di, Garr, de Buer de kumt* ("Beware, Guard, of the peasant, [who is] coming").
* The Spanish cried *Santiago* ("Saint James") both when reconquering Spain from the [Moors](/wiki/Moors "Moors") and during conquest in early colonial America.
* Polish "[Winged Hussars](/wiki/Polish_hussars "Polish hussars")" used to shout *Jezus Maria* ("[Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus"), [Mary](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus")") or *Matka Boska* ("[Mother of God](/wiki/Mother_of_God "Mother of God")") during their charges.
* King [Henry IV of France](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France") (1553–1610), a pleasure-loving and cynical military leader, was famed for wearing a striking white plume in his helmet and for his [war cry](/wiki/War_cry "War cry"): *Ralliez-vous à mon panache blanc!* ("Follow my white plume!").
* Burmese soldiers of the [Konbaung Dynasty](/wiki/Konbaung_Dynasty "Konbaung Dynasty") under [Alaungpaya](/wiki/Alaungpaya "Alaungpaya") were recorded to shout *Shwebo-Thar* (Sons of Shwebo) during the [Konbaung-Hanthawady War](/wiki/Konbaung%E2%80%93Hanthawaddy_War "Konbaung–Hanthawaddy War").
* Most of the *[jaikaras](/wiki/Jaikara "Jaikara")* were popularized by [Guru Gobind Singh](/wiki/Guru_Gobind_Singh "Guru Gobind Singh"). The [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") have a number of battle cries or jaikara: the most popular ones are as follows:
  + *Bolna[ji](/wiki/-ji "-ji")* (Respectfully Utter) – *Waheguru* ([Wondrous Enlightener](/wiki/Waheguru "Waheguru"))
  + *[Deg Teg Fateh](/wiki/Deg_Tegh_Fateh "Deg Tegh Fateh")* (Victory to Charity and Arms) – *Birdh ki Paij* *Panth ki jit* ([Rout](/wiki/Rout "Rout") of the Enemy Victory of the Sikh [Path](/wiki/Panth "Panth"))
  + *[Bole So Nihal](/wiki/Bole_So_Nihal "Bole So Nihal")...[Sat Sri Akaal](/wiki/Sat_Sri_Akaal "Sat Sri Akaal")* ("Shout Aloud in Ecstasy... True is the Great [Timeless One](/wiki/Akal_Purakh "Akal Purakh")"),
  + *Nanak naam [charhdi kalaa](/wiki/Charhdi_Kala "Charhdi Kala")* (May [Thy](/wiki/God_in_Sikhism "God in Sikhism") name be exalted O [Nanak](/wiki/Nanak "Nanak")) – *Tere* *bhaanae sarbatt daa bhalaa* (and all people prosper by Thy grace)
  + *[Waheguru ji ka Khalsa](/wiki/Waheguru "Waheguru")* (the Community of the [Pure](/wiki/Khalsa "Khalsa") belongs to the Wondrous Enlightener) – [*Waheguru ji ki Fateh*](/wiki/Waheguru_Ji_Ka_Khalsa_Waheguru_Ji_Ki_Fateh "Waheguru Ji Ka Khalsa Waheguru Ji Ki Fateh") (Victory belongs to the Wondrous Enlightener)
  + *[Raj Karega Khalsa](/wiki/Raj_Karega_Khalsa "Raj Karega Khalsa")* – (The Khalsa will rule) Aaakki Rahe Na Koye (And no opponent will remain)[[12]](#cite_note-12)
  + *Gaj ke jaikara gajaave fateh paave nihaal ho jaavae, Sat Sri Akaal gurbaar akaal hee akaal*|| (excerpt from [ardas](/wiki/Ardas "Ardas") of *[Buddha Dal](/wiki/Nihang "Nihang")* prayerbook (gutka) praising the blessing of uttering jaikara proclaiming supreme truth).
* The [Pashtun](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns") soldiers' war cry against the [Mughals](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") was *Hu, Hu*.[[13]](#cite_note-borah-13)
* The [Gurkha](/wiki/Gurkha "Gurkha") (Gorkha) soldiers' battle cry was, and still is, *"Jai Mahakali, Ayo Gorkhali!"* ("Victory to Goddess [Mahakali](/wiki/Mahakali "Mahakali"), the Gurkhas are coming!")[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* The "[rebel yell](/wiki/Rebel_yell "Rebel yell")" was a battle cry used by [Confederate](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") soldiers during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War").
* Finnish light cavalry troops in the [Swedish Army](/wiki/Swedish_Army "Swedish Army") in the 17th and 18th centuries would use the battle cry *"Hakkaa päälle!"* ("Cut them down!" in Finnish), lending them the name [Hackapell](/wiki/Hackapell "Hackapell").
* Irish regiments of various armies used and continue to use Gaelic War cries, *"[Faugh a Ballagh](/wiki/Faugh_a_Ballagh "Faugh a Ballagh")"* ("Clear the way!") or "Erin go Bragh" ("Ireland Forever")
* The [Swedish army](/wiki/Swedish_army "Swedish army") in the 18th and 19th centuries would be issued with the command to attack with "För Fäderneslandet, gå på, Hurra!" ("For the Fatherland, onwards, Hurrah!")[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* [Argentine](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") general [José de San Martín](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_de_San_Mart%C3%ADn "José de San Martín") is known in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America") for his [war cry](/wiki/War_cry "War cry"): "*Seamos libres, que lo demás no importa nada!*" ("Let's be free, nothing else matters!").
* In the [Texas Revolution](/wiki/Texas_Revolution "Texas Revolution"), following the [Battle of Goliad](/wiki/Battle_of_Goliad "Battle of Goliad") and the [Battle of the Alamo](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Alamo "Battle of the Alamo"), Texan soldiers would use the battle cry "Remember Goliad! Remember the Alamo!"
* In the [Battle of Dybbøl](/wiki/Battle_of_Dybb%C3%B8l "Battle of Dybbøl") in 1864, both Danish and German forces used "Hurrah" as a war cry.
* During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") in the [Italian Front](/wiki/Italian_Front_(World_War_I) "Italian Front (World War I)") of 1915. Before battle, Italian soldiers would yell "Savoia" or "Avanti Savoia", which is "Come on [Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy")!" or "Onwards Savoy!" in Italian (compare "For the king!" among British soldiers of the same era).

### Modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Battle_cry&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Modern")]

* During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), *Tennōheika Banzai* (天皇陛下万歳, *May the Emperor live for [ten thousand years](/wiki/Ten_thousand_years "Ten thousand years")*) served as a battle cry of sorts for Japanese soldiers, particularly in a "[banzai charge](/wiki/Banzai_charge "Banzai charge")".[[17]](#cite_note-17) The most popular battle cry is "Ei ei oh" (エイエイオー), which is usually used at the start of battle.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)
* "Avanti Savoia!" (English: Go [Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy")!) was the patriotic battle cry of the [Italian Royal Army](/wiki/Italian_Royal_Army "Italian Royal Army") during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and to a lesser extent, World War II. Infantrymen would scream this motto when launching an offensive attack, namely against the [Austro-Hungarians](/wiki/Austro-Hungarians "Austro-Hungarians").
* During the [Greco-Italian War](/wiki/Greco-Italian_War "Greco-Italian War") (in WWII), the Greeks would shout "Αέρα!" (wind) as their battle cry.
* During the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), the [Korean People's Army](/wiki/Korean_People%27s_Army "Korean People's Army") used the phrase "김일성 수령님 만세" (transliterated *gim-ilseong sulyeongnim manse*, translated as "Long live the Great Leader [Kim Il Sung](/wiki/Kim_Il_Sung "Kim Il Sung")").
* "[Hooah](/wiki/Hooah "Hooah")" is the war cry of the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army"), the [United States Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force"), and the [United States Space Force](/wiki/United_States_Space_Force "United States Space Force"). "[Oorah](/wiki/Oorah "Oorah")" is the war cry used by [United States Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps"). "[Hooyah](/wiki/Hooyah "Hooyah")" is the war cry of the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") and the [United States Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard").
* The Slavic version, "Ura!" has been used by the [Imperial Russian Army](/wiki/Imperial_Russian_Army "Imperial Russian Army"), the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army"), [Russian Ground Forces](/wiki/Russian_Ground_Forces "Russian Ground Forces"), alongside many more Eastern European armed forces. It was also used by [Yugoslav Partisans](/wiki/Yugoslav_Partisans "Yugoslav Partisans") as Juriš/Јуриш.
* "Merdeka atau mati!" (English: Freedom or death!) Used by Indonesian national army and freedom fighters (Indonesian: Pedjoeang/pejuang) during the [Indonesian National Revolution](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Revolution "Indonesian National Revolution").
* In Afghanistan, Norwegian troops of the [Telemark Battalion](/wiki/Telemark_Battalion "Telemark Battalion") would sometimes use the battle cry "Til Valhall!" (To [Valhalla](/wiki/Valhalla "Valhalla")!) followed by [Oorah](/wiki/Oorah "Oorah").[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* [Mujahideen](/wiki/Mujahideen "Mujahideen") and [jihadists](/wiki/Jihadism "Jihadism") in the Middle East and North Africa shout "[Allahu Akbar](/wiki/Takbir "Takbir")" (God is the Greatest).
* During the [Bangladesh Liberation War](/wiki/Bangladesh_Liberation_War "Bangladesh Liberation War") the [Mukti Bahini](/wiki/Mukti_Bahini "Mukti Bahini") would shout "[Joy Bangla](/wiki/Joy_Bangla_(slogan) "Joy Bangla (slogan)")" ("Victory to [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal")" / "Long live Bengal") during their battles against the Pakistani military.
* During the [Indochina War](/wiki/Indochina_War "Indochina War") in [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), the [Viet Minh](/wiki/Viet_Minh "Viet Minh") soldiers usually used "Xung phong" (English: Charge) whenever attacking the enemy. The same battle cry would be used later by [PAVN](/wiki/People%27s_Army_of_Vietnam "People's Army of Vietnam") and [NLF](/wiki/Viet_Cong "Viet Cong") forces during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War").
* Each individual regiment of the [Indian Army](/wiki/Indian_Army "Indian Army") has a unique war cry. These chants are sonorous with the beliefs of individual castes and religions from which the troops belong to.
* Croatian armed forces during the [Croatian War of Independence](/wiki/Croatian_War_of_Independence "Croatian War of Independence") would use "[Za dom spremni](/wiki/Za_dom_spremni "Za dom spremni")". One would start by saying "Za dom!" and the rest would follow with "Spremni!". It translates loosely to "For home – ready!" or "For homeland – ready!".
* [Leeroy Jenkins](/wiki/Leeroy_Jenkins "Leeroy Jenkins") is an [internet meme](/wiki/Internet_meme "Internet meme"), which has often served the purpose of an ironic or fatalistic parody of a battle cry, originating from a famous video of a [World of Warcraft](/wiki/World_of_Warcraft "World of Warcraft") clan creating an intricate battle plan, before being party-wiped as a result of Leeroy Jenkins, who had been [AFK](/wiki/Away_from_keyboard "Away from keyboard") at the time, charging in head-first. This gave rise to the phrase in [first-person shooter](/wiki/First-person_shooter "First-person shooter") games, where players will shout "LEEROOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOY JEEEEEENKIIIIIIIIIINS!" as they charge at the enemy. The phrase has gained notoriety, making appearances in various non-World of Warcraft media.
* Various [Kurdish groups](/wiki/Kurdish_nationalism "Kurdish nationalism") such as [PKK](/wiki/Kurdistan_Workers%27_Party "Kurdistan Workers' Party"), [YPG](/wiki/People%27s_Defense_Units "People's Defense Units") and [Peshmerga](/wiki/Peshmerga "Peshmerga") use the following phrases "Bijî Kurdistan!" short for "Her Bijî Kurdistan" (English: Long Live Kurdistan), which is usually accompanied with "Her Bijî!" (English : Long live) "Yan Kurdistan Yan Neman" (English: Either Kurdistan or Extinction!)
* During the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), "Slava Ukraini" ([Слава Україні!](/wiki/Slava_Ukraini "Slava Ukraini") English: Glory to Ukraine!), often accompanied by a reply of "Heroiam Slava" ([Героям слава!](/wiki/%D0%93%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BE%D1%8F%D0%BC_%D1%81%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%B0! "Героям слава!") English: Glory to the heroes!), became an internationally-known battle cry not only among domestic forces, but also among [Western](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), mostly [NATO allies](/wiki/NATO "NATO").

## In Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Battle_cry&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: In Literature")]

* In [Brian Jacques](/wiki/Brian_Jacques "Brian Jacques")'s [Redwall](/wiki/Redwall "Redwall") series, the [hares](/wiki/Hare "Hare") of the Long Patrol have the warcry "Eulalia!"
